name: Euler method uniform convergence theorem
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
The theorem concerns the convergence of the Euler method for solving an initial value problem. A sequence of approximations \(y_n\) is constructed using the Euler method with stepsizes bounded by \(H_n\) and initial errors bounded by \(K_n\), both tending to zero as \(n \to \infty\). The function \(f\) satisfies a Lipschitz condition.

Variables:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \(y\): the exact solution to the initial value problem.
    \item \(y_n\): approximation \(n\), computed using the Euler method starting from an approximation to \(y(x_0)\) with a sequence of positive steps.
    \item \(x_0\): initial \(x\)-value for the ODE.
    \item \(x\): the final \(x\)-value (or a generic point in the interval).
    \item \(f\): the function in the ODE \(y' = f(x, y)\), assumed to satisfy a Lipschitz condition.
    \item \(H_n\): an upper bound on the greatest stepsize for approximation \(n\), with \(H_n \to 0\) as \(n \to \infty\).
    \item \(K_n\): a bound on the initial error \(\|y(x_0) - y_n(x_0)\|\), with \(K_n \to 0\) as \(n \to \infty\).
\end{itemize}

variables.name: y
variables.definition: the exact solution to the initial value problem

variables.name: y_n
variables.definition: approximation number n, computed using the Euler method starting from an approximation to y(x0) with a sequence of positive steps

variables.name: x
variables.definition: the final x-value (or a generic point in the interval, but in the theorem it appears as the endpoint of the interval [x0, x])

variables.name: f
variables.definition: the function in the ODE y' = f(x, y), assumed to satisfy a Lipschitz condition

variables.name: H_n
variables.definition: an upper bound on the greatest stepsize for approximation n, with H_n → 0 as n → ∞

variables.name: K_n
variables.definition: a bound on the initial error ||y(x0) - y_n(x0)||, with K_n → 0 as n → ∞

preamble: The theorem concerns the convergence of the Euler method for solving an initial value problem. A sequence of approximations y_n is constructed using the Euler method with stepsizes bounded by H_n and initial errors bounded by K_n, both tending to zero as n increases. The function f satisfies a Lipschitz condition.
